# NIST SSDF v1.1 Glossary

**Artifact** — A piece of evidence; records generated by toolchain or process steps that provide proof of secure software development practices having been performed. (Ch 2, PO.3.3)

**Attack Surface Mapping** — A risk modelling technique used at design time to enumerate all points where an attacker could interact with the software and assess associated risk. (Ch 4, PW.1.1)

**Authorising Official Commitment** — Upper-management endorsement of secure software development, formally communicated to all personnel with development-related roles and responsibilities. (Ch 2, PO.2.3)

**Automated Delivery of Remediations** — A trusted, automated mechanism for distributing patches and updates to acquirers, enabling a single remediation to address multiple vulnerabilities. (Ch 5, RV.2.2)

**Class-Level Remediation** — Proactive identification and correction of all instances of a vulnerability's root cause within the codebase rather than waiting for each instance to be reported externally. (Ch 5, RV.3.3)

**Clean Build** — A build discipline in which all compiler warnings are treated as errors and eliminated (except confirmed false positives), producing a warning-free executable. (Ch 4, PW.6.2)

**Code Analysis** — Automated examination of human-readable source code or bytecode to find vulnerabilities and coding standard violations without executing the code; also called static analysis. (Ch 4, PW.7.1)

**Code Owner** — A designated individual or team responsible for a project's codebase, with authority to review and approve all changes made by others. (Ch 2, PO.2.1)

**Code Review** — A human-performed examination of source code to identify security vulnerabilities, backdoors, malicious content, and deviations from secure coding standards. (Ch 4, PW.7.1)

**Code Signing** — Applying a digital signature to executable files using a certificate-authority-issued certificate so that consumers can verify authenticity and integrity before execution. (Ch 3, PS.1.1)

**Commit Signing** — Cryptographically signing commits in a version control system to establish accountability to an individual account and detect unauthorised code changes. (Ch 3, PS.1.1)

**Configuration-as-Code** — Storing toolchain and environment configuration in version-controlled repositories, subject to the same access controls and integrity protections as source code. (Ch 2, PO.3.2; Ch 3, PS.1.1)

**Environment Separation** — Network-segmented, access-controlled isolation of development, build, staging, test, and distribution environments to reduce attack surface and limit lateral movement. (Ch 2, PO.5.1)

**Fuzz Testing** — An automated technique that sends malformed or unexpected inputs to software to discover input-handling vulnerabilities, crashes, and edge-case failures. (Ch 4, PW.8.2)

**Key Performance Indicator (KPI)** — A measurable value indicating how effectively security risk is being managed within the software development process. (Ch 2, PO.4.1)

**Key Risk Indicator (KRI)** — A measurable value that signals increasing risk exposure in the software development process, used alongside KPIs to track security check criteria. (Ch 2, PO.4.1)

**Least Privilege** — The principle of restricting access to code, environments, tools, and data to the minimum necessary for authorised personnel, tools, and services to perform their functions. (Ch 3, PS.1.1)

**Notional Implementation Example** — An illustrative, non-prescriptive example of a tool, process, or method that could implement an SSDF task; no example or combination of examples is required. (Ch 1)

**Pipeline-as-Code / Toolchain-as-Code** — Version-controlled specification of CI/CD pipeline configuration enabling reproducible, auditable builds and reducing configuration drift. (Ch 2, PO.3.2)

**Practice** — A named, uniquely identified SSDF activity (e.g., PO.1, PW.5) that explains what to do and why; each practice contains tasks, notional implementation examples, and references. (Ch 1)

**Product Security Incident Response Team (PSIRT)** — A dedicated team with defined processes for receiving vulnerability reports, coordinating remediation, and managing stakeholder communications. (Ch 5, RV.1.3)

**Provenance** — The chronology of origin, development, ownership, location, and changes to a system or system component and associated data, including personnel and processes involved. (Ch 3, PS.3.2)

**Risk-Based Approach** — Selecting and prioritising SSDF practices based on factors such as risk, cost, feasibility, and applicability rather than applying all practices uniformly. (Ch 1)

**Root Cause Analysis** — Post-vulnerability investigation to determine the underlying development or process failure that introduced a vulnerability, documented for developer reference and SDLC improvement. (Ch 5, RV.3.1)

**Secure by Design** — The principle that security requirements and risk responses are addressed during software architecture and design, not retrofitted after implementation. (Ch 4, PW.1)

**Secure Coding Practices** — Language-appropriate coding rules including input validation, output encoding, avoidance of unsafe functions, error handling, and logging; applied throughout source code creation. (Ch 4, PW.5.1)

**Secure Default Configuration** — A defined configuration baseline where every security-relevant setting's default value does not weaken the security functions provided by the platform or dependent services. (Ch 4, PW.9.1)

**Security Advisory** — A formal notification to software acquirers describing a vulnerability, how to identify affected instances, and how to remediate or work around it. (Ch 5, RV.2.2)

**Security Requirements (Infrastructure)** — Policies specifying how the software development infrastructure itself must be secured throughout the SDLC; reviewed at least annually or after major incidents. (Ch 2, PO.1.1)

**Security Requirements (Software)** — Policies specifying the security properties that organisation-developed software must achieve, including risk-based architecture, archiving, and end-of-life requirements. (Ch 2, PO.1.2)

**Security Response Playbook** — Pre-defined procedures for handling specific vulnerability scenarios including generic disclosures, zero-days, actively exploited vulnerabilities, and multi-party incidents. (Ch 5, RV.1.3)

**Shared Responsibility Model** — A contractual allocation of SSDF practice responsibilities between a platform/service provider and the tenant organisation when software is delivered as a service. (Ch 1)

**Shifting Left** — The principle of addressing security concerns as early as possible in the SDLC; earlier detection systematically reduces remediation cost and technical debt. (Ch 1)

**Software Acquirer** — A federal agency or other organisation that procures software and can use the SSDF to define security requirements in acquisition processes. (Ch 1)

**Software Bill of Materials (SBOM)** — A formal, machine-readable inventory of all components in a software release, enabling supply-chain risk visibility and post-release vulnerability analysis. (Ch 3, PS.3.2)

**Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC)** — A formal or informal methodology for designing, creating, and maintaining software; SSDF practices apply regardless of which SDLC model is used. (Ch 1)

**Software Producer** — An organisation that develops software, including COTS vendors, GOTS developers, custom software developers, and internal development teams. (Ch 1)

**Standardised Security Features** — Reuse of existing, vetted modules for security functions (logging, identity management, access control, cryptography) rather than creating proprietary implementations. (Ch 4, PW.1.3)

**Static Analysis** — See Code Analysis. (Ch 4, PW.7)

**Task** — A specific action within an SSDF practice that may be needed to accomplish the practice's goal; identified by a compound ID such as PO.1.1 or PW.7.2. (Ch 1)

**Temporary Mitigation** — An interim workaround applied while a permanent vulnerability fix is developed; must be communicated to acquirers with a timeline for a permanent solution. (Ch 5, RV.2.2)

**Threat Modelling** — A design-time risk modelling technique that identifies potential threats and attack scenarios so that mitigations can be built into the software architecture. (Ch 4, PW.1.1)

**Third-Party Component Verification** — Ongoing confirmation that acquired software components remain compliant with security requirements and free of newly disclosed vulnerabilities throughout their life cycles. (Ch 4, PW.4.4)

**Vulnerability Disclosure Program** — A formal programme enabling security researchers to report potential vulnerabilities to the software producer, backed by a published policy and PSIRT. (Ch 5, RV.1.3)

**Zero-Trust Architecture** — A hosting infrastructure model (per NIST SP 800-207) requiring continuous verification of identity and authorisation rather than implicit trust based on network location; applied to development environments under PO.5. (Ch 2, PO.5.1)
